summary: Joel Spolsky describes how to prioritize features for a software product, using FogBugz 5.0 as a case study. He first warns against two common prioritization mistakes: building features promised to individual customers (which leads to consultingware) and doing tasks just because they're 'inevitable' (which is procrastination in disguise). He then presents a practical group prioritization method learned from Excel 5 planning: list features on cards, vote to eliminate unpopular ones, assign rough costs, then give everyone fake money to 'buy' the features they care about most. Dividing total spend by cost produces a ranked priority list that reflects collective wisdom. The whole process took three hours and produced a list the team felt genuinely reflected the right priorities.
key_points: Building features promised to individual customers pushes you toward consultingware and away from the profitable shrinkwrap model with zero marginal costs
Doing tasks simply because they're 'inevitable' is procrastination disguised as productivity — importance is analog, not binary
A lightweight group prioritization method using fake money allocation and cost estimates produces surprisingly good results in just a few hours
The prioritization doesn't need to be precise — it only needs to roughly separate small, medium, and large features to guide development order
Letting less-important things go undone is a sign of effective prioritization, not negligence
money_quotes: If you ever find yourself implementing a feature simply because it has been promised to one customer, RED DANGER LIGHTS should be going off in your head.

Custom development is that murky world where a customer tells you what to build, and you say, 'are you sure?' and they say yes, and you make an absolutely beautiful spec, and they sign it in blood, and then you build that thing, and they are horrified and shocked.
I found myself pretending that all non-optional tasks were equally important, and therefore, since they were inevitable anyway, they could be done in any order!
More importantly, I should have realized that 'important' is not a binary thing, it's an analog thing.

If you want to get things done, you positively have to understand at any given point in time what is the most important thing to get done right now and if you're not doing it, you're not making progress at the fastest possible rate.
I've grown to think that keeping your desk clean is actually probably a sign that you're not being effective.
The roughly 50% of the features that we didn't have time to do were really stupid features, and Excel was better because it didn't have them.
Shrinkwrap has no marginal costs for each additional customer, so you can essentially sell the same thing over and over again and make a lot more profit.
key_insight: Effective feature prioritization requires ruthlessly distinguishing what matters most right now from what merely seems inevitable, and a simple group exercise with fake-money allocation beats both sales-driven promises and procrastination-driven busywork.
